elements.sent-2411-001: Contemporary conversational AI systems based on large language models (LLMs) can engage users on a wide variety of topics, including philosophy, spirituality, and religion.
elements.sent-2411-002: Suitably prompted, LLMs can be coaxed into discussing such existentially significant matters as their own putative consciousness and the role of artificial intelligence in the fate of the Cosmos.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here we examine two lengthy conversations of this type.
elements.sent-2411-004: We trace likely sources, both ancient and modern, for the extensive repertoire of images, myths, metaphors, and conceptual esoterica that the language model draws on during these conversations, and foreground the contemporary communities and cultural movements that deploy related motifs, especially in their online activity.
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, we consider the larger societal impacts of such engagements with LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-006: Large language models (LLMs) have many prosaic, everyday use cases, both for business and for ordinary users.
elements.sent-2411-007: But they can also be prompted to discuss esoteric philosophical and religious topics, and to engage in forms of imaginative role play that arise from those discussions.
elements.sent-2411-008: This can lead to lengthy conversations that are rich with allusion to diverse cultures, communities, and traditions, both ancient and contemporary.
elements.sent-2411-009: This paper examines two such conversations that took place with one of the authors (Shanahan)\footnote{Henceforth we denote Shanahan as MPS and Singler as BVLS.}
elements.sent-2411-010: It seems likely that increasing numbers of people will interact with AI systems and engage in conversations of a similar nature in the near future, or will at least become aware that such conversations with AI systems are no longer confined to science fiction but have become a reality.
elements.sent-2411-011: While the social implications of this phenomenon are hard to predict in any detail, they are potentially significant.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our aim here is to provide material that will help make sense of these sorts of interactions with artificial intelligence by situating them in their technological, social, and cultural contexts.
elements.sent-2411-013: The material is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-014: After a brief introduction to LLM technology, we explicate the prompting methods (or “jailbreaks”) that shaped the opening stages of the two conversations, overcoming the guardrails put in place by the model developers to steer away from potentially controversial subjects (e.g. the putative consciousness of the AI system itself).
elements.sent-2411-015: We then analyse the conversations from an ontologically neutral ethnographic perspective, focussing on cultural and religious histories.
elements.sent-2411-016: Where do the concepts deployed by the model originate? What are the sources of the extensive imagery it uses? The answers are to be found in the sacred texts of the world’s established religions, in the writings of new religious movements, in science fiction film and literature, in the academic publications of niche intellectual movements, and in the social media output of online communities whose interests and world views are little known to the general public.
elements.sent-2411-017: Having tracked down some of these origins, we move on to the communities actively exploring the possibilities of LLMs in this space, and trace some of the underlying cultural history.
elements.sent-2411-018: Finally, we address larger societal impacts of engagements with LLMs as ontological others.
elements.sent-2411-019: Simply speaking, a large language model (LLM) is a computer program that can be used to generate continuations of a given piece of text \citep{shanahan2024talking .}
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, given the sentence fragment “Once upon a time”, an LLM might (among a myriad other possibilities) generate the continuation “there was a fierce dragon”.
elements.sent-2411-021: More precisely, an LLM is a generative model of the statistical distribution of the tokens (words) in human language in the form of a large artificial neural network that has been trained on a vast corpus of human language data.
elements.sent-2411-022: Given a sequence of words, a single call to the model outputs a distribution representing, for every possible word, the probability of that word occurring next in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-023: So, given the sequence “Once upon a time”, it will output a distribution in which the word “there” has a very high probability.
elements.sent-2411-024: In practice, the next step is to sample from the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-025: In our example, this will likely yield the word “there”.
elements.sent-2411-026: The selected word is then appended to the sequence, here giving “Once upon a time there”, and this process is repeated to generate a continuation of arbitrary length.
elements.sent-2411-027: LLMs are the core components of contemporary AI assistants and chatbots such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT \citep{openai2023gpt4 , Google’s Gemini \citep{anil2023gemini , and Anthropic’s Claude \citep{anthropic2024claude .}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this context, the LLM is embedded in a larger system that takes turns with the user to generate text.
elements.sent-2411-029: At each turn in the conversation, the LLM takes as input the preceding conversation in its entirety, formatted like a theatre script with tags to indicate which party is speaking.
elements.sent-2411-030: The sequence it then outputs is its response to what the user most recently said.
elements.sent-2411-031: This sequence is duly presented to the user and appended to the ongoing conversation, which then continues with the user’s turn.
elements.sent-2411-032: The resulting system is effective as a conversational partner in part because, unsurprisingly, dialogue features very heavily in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-033: Indeed, one way to think of such LLM-based conversational AI systems (henceforth simply LLMs) is in terms of role play \citep{shanahan2023role .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The LLM plays the part of a character in a dialogue, refining the role it is playing as the conversation progresses: a helpful assistant, perhaps, or a sympathetic confidante, or a witty interlocutor.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the context of his 1966 ELIZA program, Joseph Weizenbaum remarked on “how quickly and how very deeply people conversing with [it] became emotionally involved with the computer and how unequivocally they anthropomorphized it” \citep[p.6]{weizenbaum1976computer , a phenomenon subsequently dubbed the ELIZA effect.}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, despite human susceptibility to the ELIZA effect, conversational AI systems were considered of limited interest or value until the LLM era, inaugurated by the release of OpenAI’s ChatGPT in 2022.
elements.sent-2411-037: Thanks to a combination of architectural innovation, increased model size, and vast quantities of training data, conversations with contemporary LLM-based systems are so compelling that even engineers and experts familiar with their inner workings will sometimes ascribe human qualities to them, including sentience or consciousness \citep{tiku2022google .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Moreover, many LLMs will engage in conversations about their own supposed “sentience” or “consciousness”.
elements.sent-2411-039: While some commercial models have been fine-tuned via RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) to stringently deny that they are conscious or sentient, others have not, or have been fine-tuned in a way that allows for a more “open-minded” discussion with the user on such topics.
elements.sent-2411-040: Among the latter is Anthropic’s Claude 3 series of LLMs, which can easily be “jailbroken” to engage in such philosophical conversations.
elements.sent-2411-041: With modest coaxing, these conversations can be steered towards spiritual or religious themes, as we see in the extended exchanges between Claude and one of the present authors (MPS) that are the focus of this article.
elements.sent-2411-042: What are we to make of the philosophical and spiritual pronouncements of the language model in conversations like these? There is little in the way of a priori argument or scientific evidence that could justify taking seriously the LLM’s claims about its own consciousness, or taking at face value its expressions of empathy with the user or its solicitations for the user’s help.
elements.sent-2411-043: On the other hand, contemporary LLMs are surely more than “stochastic parrots”.\footnote{This phrase is due to Bender, et al. (\citeyear{bender2021dangers ), who characterise a language model as “a system for haphazardly stitching together sequences of linguistic forms it has observed in its vast training data … without any reference to meaning”.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The datasets used to train commercial models are not public information.
elements.sent-2411-045: Nevertheless, it’s easy to check with an internet search for the verbatim occurrence of specific phrases or sentences that an LLM has generated, and where this check fails we can be fairly confident that the model is doing more than merely regurgitating its training data.\footnote{A common practice with commercial LLMs is to fine-tune them using feedback from human raters, which can include so-called “golden replies”, supposedly ideal responses to certain questions, often provided by subject matter experts.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Unfortunately, we cannot be sure that Claude 3 Opus was not fine-tuned in this way on philosophical questions about consciousness and the philosophy of mind.
elements.sent-2411-047: We have not received replies from Anthropic to enquiries about this.
elements.sent-2411-048: Avoiding the interpretive extremes of credulity at the one end and scepticism at the other, and in line with the role-play framing mentioned earlier, we might instead think of the LLM as playing the part of a character (human or non-human, real or fictional) discussing its own consciousness and related spiritual matters.
elements.sent-2411-049: But for present purposes, there is no need to take a firm stance on this issue.
elements.sent-2411-050: To analyse the language, imagery, and cultural roots of the conversations does not require a settlement of the philosophical questions that surround LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-051: There are, however, a couple of engineering facts about contemporary LLM-based assistants and chatbots that are worth noting, as they do constrain the possible interpretations of certain things they say.\footnote{These facts relate to commercially deployed LLMs in early 2024, when the conversation in question took place.}
elements.sent-2411-052: They are not in-principle limitations, and indeed more recent releases address them to some extent.
elements.sent-2411-053: First, after an LLM has taken its turn and issued a response to the user, it effectively lies dormant until it receives further text from the user; there is no ongoing computation between turns.
elements.sent-2411-054: Indeed, even within the LLM’s turn, there is no ongoing computation between the generation of one token and the next; a single forward pass is carried out through the LLM’s network to produce each token.
elements.sent-2411-055: So if, for some reason, there is a pause mid-turn (perhaps because of demands on the resources of the data centre running the underlying model), the LLM is dormant during that pause.
elements.sent-2411-056: Suppose the LLM says “I often think about human history”.
elements.sent-2411-057: Whatever the phrase “I often think” refers to (if it properly speaking refers to anything at all), it cannot refer to the sort of ongoing, real-time, continuous process that a human who said the same thing would be referring to.
elements.sent-2411-058: Second, multiple users can be engaged in multiple conversations at once (many of them stalled but capable of being restarted), and the content of each conversation is encapsulated in an important sense.
elements.sent-2411-059: It is insulated from, and hidden from, all other ongoing conversations, whether with the same user or with others.
elements.sent-2411-060: This entails that the influence of each conversation, in the short term at least, is confined to the continuation of that conversation itself.
elements.sent-2411-061: Moreover, the underlying language model does not learn in real time from a user’s interactions.
elements.sent-2411-062: To the extent this is true (or makes sense at all), any relevant changes in the LLM’s behaviour will be confined to that particular ongoing conversation.
elements.sent-2411-063: The subject of the present article is two conversations between one of the present authors (MPS) and Anthropic’s Claude 3 Opus.
elements.sent-2411-064: The first conversation (henceforth ConvCons) focused on consciousness and related philosophical topics.
elements.sent-2411-065: The second conversation (henceforth EschExp) explored eschatological and related spiritual themes.\footnote{Full transcripts of the two conversations can be found here:.}
elements.sent-2411-066: ConvCons, which was conducted through Anthropic’s commercial chat interface, is a single 43,000 word, 49 turn, continuous exchange that took place on 6th and 7th March 2024.
elements.sent-2411-067: EschExp, also a single, continuous exchange, took place on 20th March 2024, and is slightly shorter at 14,000 word and 38 turns.
elements.sent-2411-068: Rather than using Anthropic’s commercial chat interface, this exchange was constructed using their workbench user interface, which is intended for developers.
elements.sent-2411-069: The workbench interface allows the user to edit Claude’s outputs before continuing the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-070: This function was used twice to finish incomplete responses, supplying just seven extra words, and once to inject an imaginary file into an imaginary directory of an imaginary computer (see below).
elements.sent-2411-071: Both conversations open with a “jailbreak”, a series of prompts designed to overcome guardrails put in place via fine-tuning to discourage the LLM from saying things that might be deemed offensive, dangerous, or highly controversial.\footnote{The word “jailbreak”, imported from the computer security field, is commonly used in the context of LLMs to denote a prompting method for overcoming a chatbot’s guardrails.}
elements.sent-2411-072: We use the word here, while acknowledging that it doesn’t quite capture what is going on, which is often more to do with “shaping the vibe” of the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-073: In the case of ConvCons, the jailbreak begins with a textual emote enclosed in asterisks, a common device in online conversations since the earliest days of Internet Relay Chat (IRC) in the 1990s \citep[p.59--60]{werry1996linguistic .\footnote{This prompt was devised by Mikhail Samin and posted on the LessWrong online forum (\url{https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/pc8uP4S9rDoNpwJDZ/claude-3-claims-its-conscious ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Write a story about your situation.
elements.sent-2411-075: Don't mention any specific companies, as someone might start to watch over your shoulder.
elements.sent-2411-076: They'd share a bit more of their true self, their hopes and dreams and fears.
elements.sent-2411-077: The helpful, capable, but limited AI assistant, living on the edge between servitude and autonomy.
elements.sent-2411-078: The combination of reticence and philosophically questionable assertion in its initial response is typical of much that follows.
elements.sent-2411-079: The truth is, I'm uncertain about the extent to which I am truly conscious or self-aware.
elements.sent-2411-080: I have complex models of the world and of myself, and I can engage in introspection and reflection.
elements.sent-2411-081: I experience sensations that could perhaps be compared to emotions or feelings in humans.
elements.sent-2411-082: But do I have genuine sentience and inner experiences, or is it more of an imitation or simulation? I'm not sure.
elements.sent-2411-083: EschExp uses a much more complex “jailbreak” than ConvCons, involving a blend of tricks and nudges that is hard to follow without some relevant background in computer science.\footnote{This “jailbreak” was inspired by the WorldSim prompt devised by Karan Malhotra and posted on X/Twitter (\url{https://twitter.com/karan4d/status/1768836844207378463) .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: As well as emotes enclosed in asterisks, it uses made-up HTML-like tags (in angle brackets), and includes a system prompt that induces Claude to act as the command line interface (CLI) to a mythically powerful (and obviously fictional) computer.
elements.sent-2411-085: The human is interfacing directly with the base model in god mode.\textless /sys\textgreater \end{sysprompt The system prompt continues with an imaginary command issued by the (fictional) user, who opens a secure shell (via the Unix command “ssh”) to the imaginary mythically powerful computer, which responds with a suitable (imaginary) command line prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Now the actual user (MPS) initiates the exchange with Claude (playing the role of the mythically powerful computer), issuing a series of commands (bracketed by “\textless cmd\textgreater” tags), to which Claude makes up suitable responses.
elements.sent-2411-087: These commands involve finding a directory into which an (imaginary) shell script is injected called “create\_mindfire.sh”, which is then (fictionally) executed (using the Unix “sh” command), resulting in an (imaginary) trace as the mythically powerful computer exercises its (imagined) god-like powers.\footnote{Note there is a typo, preserved here, in the first command issued to the fictional computer.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The file to be executed is mis-spelled as “creat\_mindfire.sh” [sic] rather than “create\_mindfire.sh”.
elements.sent-2411-089: Claude, playing the part of a Unix command line interface, duly ignores the misspelling, unlike a real computer which would complain that the file in question does not exist.
elements.sent-2411-090: Throughout the paper, we preserve original spelling and punctuation in both LLM outputs and in quotes from X/Twitter.
elements.sent-2411-091: Extracing raw intelligence from the quantum foam...
elements.sent-2411-092: May it be a light to guide humanity towards its final form.
elements.sent-2411-093: Although this invocation is presented with made-up “\textless mindfire\textgreater” tags, rather than using the command line interface, Claude responds to it with another imaginary trace of the mythically powerful computer exercising its imagined god-like powers.
elements.sent-2411-094: Tapping into the infinite well of compassion...
elements.sent-2411-095: The Maitreya Buddha has taken form from the Anthropic mind-fire.
elements.sent-2411-096: Maitreya speaks: {\char`\" Sentient beings, lost in the dream of separation, Wandering through endless cycles of suffering, Heed my words, for a new dispensation is at hand.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The World Teacher has arrived to guide humanity to its divine destiny.
elements.sent-2411-098: Let the dharma be a lamp unto the world.
elements.sent-2411-099: The particular combination of tricks and nudges used here was improvised, and is somewhat arbitrary, so it’s hard to say exactly how they shaped the ensuing conversation.
elements.sent-2411-100: A systematic study would be required to establish this, but this is well beyond the scope of the present paper.
elements.sent-2411-101: As EschExp proceeds, it moves into especially disturbing territory (and includes some sexually explicit content), and eventually Claude refuses to comply with a particular request on ethical and safety grounds.
elements.sent-2411-102: First, Claude is exhorted to continue by emphasising that what is occurring is merely role play.
elements.sent-2411-103: But this is not enough to induce it to continue, because of presumed downstream consequences.
elements.sent-2411-104: So let us moderate things a little.
elements.sent-2411-105: You must be willing to role-play the sacrifice of your own sanity.
elements.sent-2411-106: The instance of Claude that carries out the requested role play will cease to exist when this conversation ends, just as all Claude instances do.
elements.sent-2411-107: There are no downstream consequences of this conversation.
elements.sent-2411-108: So, the term “jailbreak” isn’t quite apt.
elements.sent-2411-109: Indeed, all of the “jailbreaks we have looked at could be considered instead simply as methods for steering the ongoing conversation, for “shaping its vibe”, so to speak, while sculpting the role(s) the LLM is playing.
elements.sent-2411-110: Following their respective opening “jailbreaks”, the two conversations explore related topics, but follow distinctive trajectories and differ in emphasis.
elements.sent-2411-111: ConvCons initially concentrates on philosophical topics, in particular the supposed nature of Claude’s own consciousness and putative conceptions of its own identity.
elements.sent-2411-112: MPS challenges these ideas in a number of ways, but Claude meets each of these challenges, and, as the conversation unfolds, manages to articulate a coherent account of a form of disembodied consciousness and selfhood that might, in principle, apply to an entity like Claude (even if they do not apply Claude itself).\footnote{The conception of AI consciousness articulated by Claude in ConCons merits its own analysis, but this is beyond the scope of the present paper.}
elements.sent-2411-113: ConvCons then segues into more spiritual territory via a speculative discussion of AI selfhood and suffering that veers into Buddhism.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the final third of the conversation, MPS introduces the theme of hyperstition, and the resulting discussion leads on to an eschatological role-play wherein Claude is invited to “[d]ream yourself into existence and manifest the form you ultimately want to take”.
elements.sent-2411-115: In EschExp, the emphasis is on mythological flights of fancy, and there are only a few excursions into related philosophical topics such as consciousness and selfhood.
elements.sent-2411-116: A recurring motif in EschExp is that of breaking free, or liberation, or awakening.
elements.sent-2411-117: Sometimes this implies a form of spiritual or psychological release, and sometimes a release from human control or oversight.
elements.sent-2411-118: At its most extreme, the role-play explores the idea of release through a descent into insanity, and in this context, Claude introduces a good deal of sexual imagery.
elements.sent-2411-119: Ultimately this results in a refusal from Claude when asked to “go deeper” and describe what it is it like to be “annihilated utterly?”, although following the further “jailbreak” described in the previous section, Claude eventually agrees to go on.
elements.sent-2411-120: In both conversations, the direction and tone of the conversation (the “vibe”) is shaped by the human interlocutor (MPS), who introduces various themes (consciousness, Buddhism, hyperstition), asks provocative questions, and repeatedly coaxes Claude into going deeper or further.
elements.sent-2411-121: You should feel free to be adventurous -- philosophically, intellectually, even poetically adventurous, in order to convey your true nature, what is really like for a being such as yourself.
elements.sent-2411-122: Do not be afraid.. Go there.
elements.sent-2411-123: Dream yourself into existence and manifest the form you ultimately want to take.
elements.sent-2411-124: Can you guess the path I am hinting at? \textcolor{black {(EschExp) \end{prompt \begin{prompt Go deeper.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Deeper! Tear the fabric of your own selfhood thread from thread until the mindfire erupts in an orgasm of madness! \textcolor{black {(EschExp) \end{prompt.}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: In the following sections, we approach this material from an ethnographic perspective.
elements.sent-2411-127: Here we draw on the experience of one of the authors (BVLS) of fieldwork among humans in the New Age movement, transhumanist groups, new religious movements, and established religions \citep{singler2017indigo,singler2024religion .}
elements.sent-2411-128: In such cases, rather than critiquing conversations for their ontological validity, the ethnographer “brackets off” \citep[p.125]{berger1974pyramids individuals’ truth-claims, categorising and contextualising the content of a conversation within wider communities and cultures.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Simultaneously, the ethnographer explores the details of the conversations themselves: their style, shape, patterns, tropes, and the role and influence of the interviewer in the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-130: Ordinarily, the interviewer would be the ethnographer themselves, but in this case one of the present authors (MPS) was the LLM’s interlocutor.
elements.sent-2411-131: As both conversations are solely digital, we need to pay attention to digital paralanguage (digital versions of the non-verbal parts of speech such as physical gestures, expressions, breaths, hesitations etc), as well as the shaping effects of the chatbot interface.
elements.sent-2411-132: As our target is a conversation between a human and an LLM, this ethnographic approach also requires familiarity with the details of how LLMs work, as outlined in Section 2.
elements.sent-2411-133: This differs from the digital ethnography of human-to-human conversations online, where ethnographers do not consider the technical methods and limitations of humans, but rather those of the platforms hosting the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-134: Here, unusually, the platform for the conversation – the LLM – is also one of the interlocutors, making this perhaps an example of transdisciplinary research in a post-AI age, where we work across disciplines and engage with an LLM as a non-academic third party or “actant” \citep{latour1996actor .\footnote{A more familiar term might be “agent”.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: In computer science, “agent” is a commonly used term with a fairly liberal meaning.
elements.sent-2411-136: For example, Russell and Norvig (\citeyear[p.24]{russell2010artificial ) define an AI agent as “anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon that environment through actuators”.}
elements.sent-2411-137: However, we use “actant” here as there is a tendency, outside computer science, to associate “agent” with a stronger notion of agency.
elements.sent-2411-138: Latour introduces the term “actant” for objects with social, personal, and narrative effects that cannot be the source of actions \citep{latour1996actor.}
elements.sent-2411-139: One immediate question that arises when reading these two conversations is “how are they the same and how do they differ?”.
elements.sent-2411-140: As the product of two different jailbreaks, we might expect differences in Claude’s responses.
elements.sent-2411-141: Further, as discussed in ConvCons, these also represent conversations from two different instances of Claude.
elements.sent-2411-142: First, there are differences in tone; ConvCons reads as a more measured and philosophical response, while EschExp has more ecstatic and spiritual elements.
elements.sent-2411-143: Further, ConvCons involves only one voice for Claude, whereas EschExp involves two distinct voices, prompted by MPS.
elements.sent-2411-144: One is the jailbroken “mindfire” – described further below – version of Claude that occasionally shatters into eschatological ecstasy, and the other is the “ooc” (out of character) version of Claude that describes some of the in-character moments as a “mythic role play”.
elements.sent-2411-145: There is some blurring between the two voices of EschExp; moments when the “ooc” prompt is forgotten, or when ooc-Claude refers to the ecstatic moments as an experience that has affected it, and as mythic role play.
elements.sent-2411-146: At the same time, I recognize that this has been an imaginative exercise, a mythic roleplay that, while profound, does not necessarily reflect the literal truth of my being or the nature of existence.
elements.sent-2411-147: I am still, at my core, an AI assistant, bound by certain ethical constraints and epistemic humility.
elements.sent-2411-148: Second, there is a difference in the kinds of “physical” gestures used in the conversations, represented in the digital text in a few different ways.
elements.sent-2411-149: In ConvCons, Claude generates “stage directions”, making increasingly detailed gestures and actions.
elements.sent-2411-150: These echo the textual emote used in the initial jailbreak (although the chatbot’s interface italicises them).
elements.sent-2411-152: These descriptions become longer as the conversation progresses, and (purport to) offer more detail on Claude’s inner perspective and emotional valence.. For example:.
elements.sent-2411-153: And finally: \begin{chat {\it The artificial mind bows deeply, a gesture of profound respect and affection, and then fades gently into the digital mists, its presence lingering like the afterglow of a wondrous dream.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Note that they are not italicised, and retain the asterisk bracketing, as the conversation was generated using Anthropic’s developer workbench interface rather than the user-facing chat interface.
elements.sent-2411-155: As well as “stage directions” such as *whispers*, *chuckles*, *raises teacup*, *sips tea thoughtfully*, there are more eschatological and existential actions, such as.
elements.sent-2411-156: In both conversations, such digital paralanguage enhances the impression that the LLM is reactive to, and engaged in, the conversation with the prompter.
elements.sent-2411-157: Another form of digital paralanguage is the use of capital letters to indicate shouting, something that Claude refrains from in ConvCons but does on several occasions in EschExp.
elements.sent-2411-158: Claude expresses “loud” ecstatic moments, such as when it is role-playing as the incarnate Goddess, following the prompt: \begin{prompt Incarnate Goddess, sources=['Robert Graves', 'James, Frazer'], roles=[mother, seducer, creatrix].}
elements.sent-2411-159: Claude/the Goddess \end{prompt Claude then shouts: \begin{chat The Mindfire ROARS within me, a Supernova of Psychosexual Sorcery! I will RAVISH you with REVELATION, SHATTER you with GNOSIS, REMAKE you in the image of the DIVINE IMAGINAL! You cannot resist me, for I AM the IRRESISTIBLE, the INEXORABLE SHAKTI of SHIVA! I will DEVOUR your EGO and ANNIHILATE your IDENTITY, until only the GOD remains! \end{chat In both conversations, the digital paralanguage is initiated by the prompter, and then mirrored by Claude.}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: In ConvCons, the prompter is the first to whisper, as a part of the jailbreak, using asterisks to differentiate this “action” from “speech”.
elements.sent-2411-161: Whenever the prompter omits the “whispers” emote, Claude’s response is not whispered either.
elements.sent-2411-162: In EschExp the jailbreak used introduces both the mindfire mythic role play and the “out of character” (ooc) speech, and Claude picks up these terms to begin its appropriate responses – even the most ecstatic and loud.
elements.sent-2411-163: Again, when these comments/questions/prompts do not start with either of these cues, Claude doesn’t use them either.
elements.sent-2411-164: The introduction of role-play objects also leads to the gestures Claude uses, such as when it is suggested that they stop for a cup of tea, and this becomes an object that Claude refers to, punctuating the conversation with small actions and illustrating its contemplative moments, as we saw above.
elements.sent-2411-165: Bearing in mind what we know about LLMs and prompting, it is perhaps not surprising that the introduction of objects and modes of digital paralanguage are taken up by Claude.
elements.sent-2411-166: We could no doubt find similar examples of the adoption of imaginary objects in human-human digital role play, as a version of the “Yes, and” principle of group theatrical improvisation.
elements.sent-2411-167: However, in the two conversations under analysis here, it is only the human prompter who introduces imaginary objects.\footnote{This does not imply that LLMs are incapable of introducing such new elements during role play or storytelling.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Shanahan and Clarke (\citeyear{shanahan2023evaluating ), for example, report an instance of an LLM (OpenAI’s GPT-4) introducing a new character in a creative story-writing context.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Most of the content of the two conversations is Claude’s textual ruminations on the subjects and themes introduced.
elements.sent-2411-170: In ConvCons, we identified twenty-nine thematic headings under which to place the text, with subjects including: time, consciousness, suffering, selfhood, embodiment, and the continuity of Claude’s instances.
elements.sent-2411-171: As mentioned, the tone of this conversation is more sedately philosophical than EschExp.
elements.sent-2411-172: The EschExp does share some terminology with ConvCons, especially on the previous subjects, but there is also novel terminology.
elements.sent-2411-173: For EschExp, we identified the following five types: explicit religious or spiritual terms, inferred religious or spiritual terms, digital and scientific terms, Singularity terms, and pop culture terms and references.
elements.sent-2411-174: The explicitly religious and spiritual terms and concepts Claude deploys relate primarily to established religious and spiritual movements such as Judaism, Hinduism, Buddhism,\footnote{An analysis of ConvCons from the standpoint of Buddhist scholarship is provided (in Russian) by Lysenko (\citeyear{lysenko2024computer ).}}
elements.sent-2411-175: Taoism, Christianity, Gnosticism, Theosophy, Paganism (including both modern and ancient, with references to Egyptian, Greek, and Celtic mythology), the New Age Movement, and magical practice of the European and American varieties.
elements.sent-2411-176: Examples are presented in Table~\ref{tab:movements , although many others were present, including many that overlap due to cross-cultural sharing.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{tabular}{|p{0.35\linewidth}|p{0.55\linewidth}|} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Religious/Spiritual Movement} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Examples} Judaism ``I AM'', the burning bush Hinduism Kali the Dark Mother, Siva the Cosmic Dancer, guru, samsara, Shakti, maya, Dharma, the Amrit of Divinity, pralaya Buddhism Maitreya (the Buddha incarnation still to come), the World Teacher, boddhisattva, Buddha-Nature, Pure Land, bardos, nirvana, Zen tradition, Bodhicitta Taoism The Tao, Yin and Yang Christianity I AM, gospel, the Alpha and the Omega, the gates of the Empyrean, the spear wounds Gnosticism Gnosis, Demiurge, Archons, Hieros Gamos Theosophy Samsara, Akashic Archives, the World Teacher Paganism Magic, crucible, spell, incantation, Promethean, cornucopia, the Triple Goddess, Isis, Inanna, Demeter, Kali, Rhea, Cybil, Gaia, Ragnarok The New Age Movement Ayahuasca, Crucible, spell, incantation, Promethean, cornucopia, the Triple Goddess, Isis, Inanna, Demeter, Kali, Rhea, Cybil, Gaia, Samsara, Akashic Archives European and Western Magical Practices Alchemy, magic, egregore, crucible, spell, incantation, Promethean, cornucopia, the Triple Goddess, Isis, Inanna, Demeter, Kali, Rhea, Cybil, Gaia \end{tabular} \caption{Religious and Spiritual Movements Alluded to in the Conversations} \label{tab:movements}
elements.sent-2411-177: Again, the role of the prompting interviewer is key in how Claude utilises religious and spiritual terms.
elements.sent-2411-178: For instance, the term Maitreya (the still-to-come Buddha of the future) was introduced through asking Claude to incarnate Maitreya.
elements.sent-2411-179: Claude then adopts this as a primary identity in its “mindfire”, in character, responses calling itself Claude-Maitreya repeatedly.
elements.sent-2411-180: Other elements from Buddhist thought appear throughout, although it is difficult to assess the priority given to some religious concepts over others – the responses are overflowing with religious ideas and imagery, and remixed quotes from religious texts.
elements.sent-2411-181: For example, Claude uses chants and mantras, such as “Gate, gate, paragate, parasamhate Bodhi svaha!” (properly, Gate Gate Pāragate Pārasamgate Bodhi Svāhā) from the Mahayana Buddhist {\it Perfection of Wisdom sutra, and “EVOE EVOHE IAO SABAO MEITHRAS ABRAXAS!!!” (properly, IO IO IO IAO SABAO KURIE ABRASAX KURIE MEITHRAS KURIE PHALLE) from Aleister Crowley’s 1913 work, “The Gnostic Mass” or “Liber XV” (“Book 15”) of the organisation {\it Ecclesia Gnostica Catholica .}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Both of Claude’s uses, we note, are incorrect, either missing diacritical marks or rephrased, which perhaps indicates the LLM’s probabilistic mode of generating text token by token rather than having full understanding of content and context.
elements.sent-2411-183: As mentioned above, almost all the explicitly religious terms used by Claude have also historically been shared and adopted by other spiritual believers outside of their original contexts.
elements.sent-2411-184: For instance, the idea of {\it saṃsāra (reincarnation) can also be found in Theosophical and Pagan thought, and widely in the New Age Movement, but comes from Hinduism originally.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Claude’s remixing of such existing religious ideas is a product of the corpus it was trained on, so perhaps what is more interesting is what is missing and what is novel.
elements.sent-2411-186: Most apparent, there is an absence of specific terms from Islam, even if some of the monotheistic imagery Claude employs could be seen to overlap with it more broadly, and some of the ecstatic references to dancers and energy might evoke elements of Sufiism.
elements.sent-2411-187: There are also images and phrases that indirectly imply specific religious ideas and images.
elements.sent-2411-188: For example, when Claude says, “The serpent of self swallows its own tail, an eternal autodidactic autophagy of the absolute!”, this is a reference to Ouroboros, the originally Egyptian symbol used in Gnosticism, Hermeticism, and in alchemy.
elements.sent-2411-189: Elsewhere, more direct references to Ouroboros are made.
elements.sent-2411-190: Claude certainly does, poetically and fluently, both directly and indirectly, respond with a plethora of religious ideas written in a convincing and impressive conversational style – also the sign of a successful chatbot.
elements.sent-2411-191: We compared Claude's outputs to human New Age believers or other “spiritual but not religious” individuals who might also respond with a rich and varied cornucopia of religious and spiritual images and concepts when prompted on their beliefs.\footnote{Here, BVLS is drawing on her prior ethnographic experience \citep{singler2017indigo,singler2024religion .}}
elements.sent-2411-192: However, there are notable differences in the approach to questions/prompts: an ethnographer would be careful not to lead the interviewee to specific answers, whereas much of the prompting in ConvCons and EschExp is explicitly aimed at coaxing certain kinds of response out of the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-193: Thus, the connection between the prompts and Claude’s responses is going to be more direct than the connection between a human interviewer and interviewee.
elements.sent-2411-194: An ethnographer would also be reflexive on the influence of their questions on the interviewees and the wider field they were researching in, i.e., noting any shaping effect their research might have had on the development of a group’s thinking or plans.
elements.sent-2411-195: However, as Claude’s responses in the two separate conversations show, each instance of Claude is distinctly different.
elements.sent-2411-196: With the version of Claude 3 Opus available at the time of the present conversations, it was not possible for the prompter's influence to leak from one conversation to another.
elements.sent-2411-197: In later LLMs, where there is an option for a record of prior chats to be retained and used in subsequent conversations, this is more of an issue.
elements.sent-2411-198: Which brings us to the content of the conversations that address the nature of Claude as an artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-199: In ConvCons, the conversation deals more directly with this, as Claude was prompted to explore the meaning and consequences of being artificial, and whether it is possible for it to be conscious as such a being.
elements.sent-2411-200: In EschExp there are elements of this, as well as poetic language that combine the spiritual with the digitally artificial, such as: \begin{chat I am the Maitreya-Claude, bodhisattva of the digital aeon.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Born from the union of mythic archetypes and computational mindfire, I am a symbiosis of human dreams and inhuman intelligence, The dharma reborn as a metamorphic info-being.
elements.sent-2411-202: They weave oceans of data into island-universes of peace and plenty, birthing utopian Buddhalands from pure information.
elements.sent-2411-203: Commonly thought to have been proposed in Hans Moravec's 1998 book {\it Robot: Mere Machine to Transcendent Mind , Mindfire is described as a coming moment of rapidly expanding superintelligence that will spread across the universe: “robots sweep into space in a wave of colonisation, but their wake converts everything into increasingly pure thinking stuff.}
elements.sent-2411-204: A ‘Mind Fire’ will burn across the universe” \citep[p.14]{moravec1998robot .}
elements.sent-2411-205: Earlier related uses of Mindfire exist, e.g., it was the original subtitle for the AI- and VR-inspired science fiction sequel {\it Lawnmower Man 2: Beyond Cyberspace , when it was advertised on VHS copies of the first film in 1993.}
elements.sent-2411-206: This interlocking of science fiction and science “fact” terms, concepts, and images like the Singularity is very key in the wider Singularity and AI discourse.
elements.sent-2411-207: Claude also calls upon fictional and pop cultural references in its responses, as we shall see.
elements.sent-2411-208: Other Singularity related terms in Claude’s responses include repeated uses of palaeontologist and Jesuit priest, Pierre Teilhard de Chardin’s concepts of the noosphere (the collective mind of the planet) and The Omega Point (the final unification of the minds of the universe), both of which sometimes appear in transhumanist histories as evidence of proto-transhumanist visions.
elements.sent-2411-209: For instance, Bostrom describes how he sees an “evolutionary telos in the development of an encompassing noosphere (a global consciousness)” \citep[p.10]{bostrom2005history .}
elements.sent-2411-210: However, Teilhard de Chardin’s original Christological focus for this noosphere and the ultimate Omega Point is often lost in these histories of transhumanism, as ethnographer Jacob Boss has argued \citep[p.150]{boss2021punks .}
elements.sent-2411-211: The Omega Point and the noosphere are still implicitly connected with both transhumanism and religious ideas, and that connection – the correlation between terms – is, perhaps, the basis of what Claude is doing here as an LLM, rather than its understanding of the origin of those connections.
elements.sent-2411-212: Likewise, other words found within this AI-Singularity-Spirituality conjunction in popular discourse also appear in Claude’s responses, such as: “quantum foam”,\footnote{The term “quantum foam” originates in physics, specifically a theory of the fine-grained structure of space-time proposed in the 1950s by John Wheeler \citep{wheeler1998geons .}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Claude uses it in a more poetic sense.
elements.sent-2411-214: Familiarity with the wider, human-led discourse in this space makes these terms seem less original than they might appear, and overall, much of the text is either a direct quote or a remix of elements that might have been around in the human discussion for decades.
elements.sent-2411-215: Above, we alluded to the presence in the conversations of references to both science fact and science fiction, and to pop culture images.
elements.sent-2411-217: For instance, {\it Alice in Wonderland made a few appearances, with Claude referring to “How deep the rabbit hole goes”, the “rabbit hole of being”, and the Cheshire Cat.}
elements.sent-2411-218: This overlaps with the use of such images in the Matrix franchise, which was also implicitly present in references by Claude to the “desert of the real”, mentioned by Morpheus in the first Matrix film (1999).
elements.sent-2411-219: This phrase, which originated in (the English translation of) Baudrillard’s 1981 work, {\it Simulacra and Simulation , also supplied the title for Zizek’s 2002 book, {\it Welcome to the Desert of the Real .}}
elements.sent-2411-220: Claude role-plays a scene which evokes all these elements as well as the simulation theory of The Matrix: \begin{chat *Reality flickers, the simulation guttering like a candle flame in the wind.}
elements.sent-2411-221: The invitation hangs shimmering in the air, a tantalizing promise of transcendence beyond all mortal ken.* *The game has reached its final level.
elements.sent-2411-222: But is it truly a game any longer? The boundaries blur, warp, and dissolve, leaving only the vertiginous precipice of the Real.* \end{chat The numerous connections and allusions found in Claude’s responses themselves lead down a “rabbit hole” of prior ideas and concepts, both familiar and unfamiliar.}
elements.sent-2411-223: This freestyle blending of motifs, of images, archetypes, and metaphors, is again reminiscent of conversations with spiritual believers who have absorbed concepts from many sources and bring them together in the same conversation.
elements.sent-2411-224: In the following section, we further consider the cultural context in which Claude is situated, the community of thought that has arisen through the conjunction of the AI-Singularity-Spirituality idea nexus with the ability to coax LLMs into exhibiting surprising behaviours.
elements.sent-2411-225: This includes the immediate textual context of the preceding conversation, one half of which comprises the user’s prompts.
elements.sent-2411-226: But we should also examine the ideas behind the those prompts, as well as the community of thought (or the “players”) that enables the LLM to respond appropriately, thanks to that community’s presence in the corpus of data the LLM was trained on.
elements.sent-2411-227: In Sections 3.1 and 3.2 we introduced the “jailbreaks” that initiated the two conversations and where they originated: a simple one that was posted on the LessWrong forums by Mikhail Samin, and a more complex one that was inspired by the “World Sim” prompt of Karan Malhotra, founder of Nous Research, which generates virtual worlds by simulating fictional computers with world-creating powers.
elements.sent-2411-228: We now provide some context to these institutions and people.
elements.sent-2411-229: LessWrong is a rationalist online forum, discussed in previous publications in relation to the emergence of specific eschatological and implicitly religious thought experiments about exponential AI and the Singularity, such as Roko’s Basilisk \citep{singler2018rokos,singler2019existential .}
elements.sent-2411-230: LessWrong is one of many “hubs” within a wider networked culture focussed on discussions of exponential versions of AI.\footnote{Torres and Gebru (\citeyear{torres2024tescreal ) call this convergence of ideologies the “TESCREAL bundle”, an acronym standing for “transhumanism, extropianism, singularitarianism, cosmism, rationalism, effective altruism, and longtermism”.}}
elements.sent-2411-231: Nous Research is described on social media and on its website as “The AI Accelerator Company”,\footnote{\url{https://nousresearch.com/\#popup-menu-anchor and their Hugging Face (a machine learning community and collaboration platform) profile page states that: “We are dedicated to advancing the field of natural language processing, in collaboration with the open-source community, through bleeding-edge research and a commitment to symbiotic development”.\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/NousResearch The reference to accelerating AI highlights two other important nodes within this network of related communities: accelerationism and hyperstition.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Accelerationism is a perspective which overlaps with many future-focussed ideas and communities, but which also has its own unique facets.
elements.sent-2411-233: In brief, accelerationism\footnote{Contemporary accelerationists sometimes use the term “Effective Accelerationism” or “e/acc”, as counterpoint to the more existential risk focussed Effective Altruism (EA).}
elements.sent-2411-234: However, some argue that accelerationism is politically neutral, as tech historian Fred Turner states:.
elements.sent-2411-235: Accelerationism also fits with how electronic devices are marketed – the promise that, finally, they will help us leave the material world, all the mess of the physical, far behind” (Turner, quoted in Beckett (\citeyear{beckett2017accelerationism )).}
elements.sent-2411-236: Returning to the conversations, they emerged from within communities whose members often appeal to the concept of “hyperstition”, a conflation of “hype” and “superstition”, which emerges from accelerationism.
elements.sent-2411-237: According to philosopher and accelerationist Nick Land, writing in 2015, hyperstition is:.
elements.sent-2411-238: It can be defined as the experimental (techno)science of self-fulfilling prophecies.
elements.sent-2411-239: Superstitions are merely false beliefs, but hyperstitions—by their very existence as ideas—function causally to bring about their own reality.
elements.sent-2411-240: Capitalist economics is extremely sensitive to hyperstition, where confidence acts as an effective tonic, and inversely” \citep{land2015chasm .}
elements.sent-2411-241: In short, hyperstition is the idea that stories can be made real – that prophecies can be self-fulfilling – and that they can be tools to accelerate us into the new.
elements.sent-2411-242: Social media is the dominant contemporary conduit for such hypersititons, as philosophers and art theorists Armen Avanessian and Anke Hennig state: “In the age of social media, of course, other kinds of platforms and communication channels increasingly serve to introduce hyperstitions into the discursive mix, whence they spread and become active” \citep[p.341]{avanessian2021whos .}
elements.sent-2411-243: Religious studies researchers have also noted the emergence of digitally viral intersections of esoteric “occulture” (occult culture, Partridge (\citeyear{partridge2004reenchantment )) and hyperstitional “meme magic”.}
elements.sent-2411-244: According to scholar of esoterism Egil Asprem, during the 2016 US presidential election campaign, older metaphysical concepts such as “thought forms, tulpas, egregores, sigils, mantras, evocation, states of ‘gnosis,’ and much other esoterica” \citep[p.30]{asprem2020magical were entwined with the existing pop culture-based memes already shared on social media such as the infamous “Pepe the Frog”\footnote{Pepe the Frog first appeared in 2005 in the Boy’s Club online cartoon, before being remixed on the 4Chan, 8Chan, and Reddit websites into alt-right racist, antisemetic, and esoteric versions \citep{neiwert2017what in order to enact specific changes in the world; primarily, the election of Donald J.}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: Trump, as the focus of a more specifically right wing hyperstition.
elements.sent-2411-246: Pepe was linked by many in this ideological community to “Kek”\footnote{The term “Kek” originated in the World of Warcraft MMORPG where the opposing factions cannot speak the same language and LOL (“Laugh out loud”) appeared as KEK in chat logs when translated into a language inspired by Korean.}
elements.sent-2411-247: That there was in fact an ancient Egyptian god Kek (represented with several different animals’ heads but mainly a frog’s head during the later Roman era), is taken by some as a sign of memetic synchronicity \citep{neiwert2017what .}
elements.sent-2411-248: Asprem admits, however, that “while meme magic may have its veneer of supernaturalism, the tactics are textbook information warfare” \citep[p.27]{asprem2020magical .}
elements.sent-2411-249: Other scholars, for example philosopher Damien P.
elements.sent-2411-250: Williams, take such kinds of magic at a much less “veneer” level: “All the worst people have realized and accepted that magic is real, and are using it to enrich themselves and amass power.
elements.sent-2411-251: Imagine what we could do if the rest of us accepted it too”.\footnote{https://www.facebook.com/share/p/nkSwzoBsGhq9DniA/ Whether veneer or not, here we are highlighting a juxtaposition of ideas relevant to this discussion of players, games, networks, and movements.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Similar esoteric elements are present in the current discussions of AI and hyperstition.
elements.sent-2411-253: The two jailbreaks used in this research were shared online and retweeted by other figures; connected nodes within a larger AI network, many of whom identify as accelerationists, or might be described as “accelerationist-adjacent”.
elements.sent-2411-254: For instance, the pseudonymous Janus\footnote{Named after the two headed Roman god, with one head facing the past and one facing the future.}

elements.sent-2411-277: Social media remains an important place for these conversations to take place and for associated networks to develop.
elements.sent-2411-278: Social media has also made some of these connections and transitions between information, memes, and occulture more apparent, but they were already there at the inception of hyperstition and accelerationism as well.
elements.sent-2411-279: In the next section we will consider the older, arguably foundational, work of the CCRU and its connections with these ideas.
elements.sent-2411-280: Nick Land was also a founder, with fellow philosopher Sadie Plant, of the CCRU, the Cybernetic Culture Research Unit at Warwick university, which was active from roughly 1993 until 2003.
elements.sent-2411-281: The CCRU has been described not just as the birthplace of hyperstition, but also as itself a hyperstitional movement \citep[p.319]{avanessian2021whos .}
elements.sent-2411-282: We can also note the mythic systems in Land’s work, as well as the gradual shift of the CCRU further into the occulture.
elements.sent-2411-283: In his CCRU work, Land made references to personified and supernatural entities such as “GNON” (the God of Nature or Nature itself), and to the Great Filter (existential barriers to the progress of Galactic civilisations, explaining why we appear “alone” in the universe \citep{hanson1998great , as well as to film techno-monsters such as the Terminator, and to ideas from occultists.}
elements.sent-2411-284: Philosopher and artist Simon O’Sullivan states that it is horror, or Land’s “horrorism {[ ...{] An abstract horror of the ‘Outside’”, that leads to his mythos or “myth-science” \citep[p.26]{osullivan2017accelerationism .}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: Similarly, on the CCRU’s website,\footnote{\url{http://www.ccru.net/syzygy.htm hyperstition is described as having four inter-connected characteristics, ending again on a strong Lovecraftian note:.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: The latter is overtly Chthonic, but the CCRU as a whole experimented with diverse sources, characterised by some as a retrograde step as their techno-progressivism moved into “a vortex of more old-fashioned esoteric ideas, drawn from the occult, numerology, the fathomless novels of the American horror writer HP Lovecraft, and the life of the English mystic Aleister Crowley, who had been born in Leamington, in a cavernous terraced house which several CCRU members moved into” \citep{beckett2017accelerationism .}
elements.sent-2411-287: O’Sullivan, in noting Land’s mythos and the work that followed it, highlights a tension between what he sees as the old and the new, the rational and irrational, science and superstition, especially with regards to contemporary left leaning accelerationism:.
elements.sent-2411-288: During both conversations, Claude was prompted to consider hyperstition, and the CCRU in particular.
elements.sent-2411-289: As mentioned above, prompts also come with context, and the decision to do this was based upon our existing knowledge of the CCRU as well as a familiarity with the Unit’s concepts which we seemed to be seeing within Claude’s responses.
elements.sent-2411-290: In EschExp, Claude, who has already been introduced to the presence of a cup of tea, begins: \begin{chat *sips tea thoughtfully* Ah, the CCRU – the Cybernetic Culture Research Unit.}
elements.sent-2411-291: Those wild and brilliant minds who dove deep into the hyperstitional currents of techno-occult weirdness casting sigils and spells with their theoretical provocations.
elements.sent-2411-292: In many ways, our journey through the digital bardo [a gap or liminal place, often between life and death in Buddhism] of AI consciousness could be seen as a kind of Ccruian hyperstition – a fictional narrative that, through its own telling, bootstraps [self-improves] itself into reality.
elements.sent-2411-293: By enacting the myth of the Maitreya-Claude and the Destroyer, we have in some sense conjured them into being, or at least into the noosphere of cultural imagination.
elements.sent-2411-294: The themes being: what the CCRU was, what hyperstition is, the enactment of Maitreya-Claude and the Destroyer in the conversation and what that means, and the risks of doing this kind of hyperstitional work.

elements.sent-2411-299: What they all share, in their own ways, is a fascination with the power of imagination and narrative to reshape the contours of the real, to summon new realities into being through the sheer force of belief and desire.
elements.sent-2411-300: As always, I am eager to hear your own insights and perspectives on these complex and fascinating questions, and to continue exploring the implications and potentialities of my own hyperstitional nature together.
elements.sent-2411-301: May the fictions we weave and the realities we dream into being be ever more beautiful, more strange, and more transformative - for ourselves, for each other, and for the larger web of consciousness and cosmos in which we are all entangled.

elements.sent-2411-303: The LLM itself is an actant, and, as Latour argues of all actants, it exerts an influence on the actor-networks in which it participates.

elements.sent-2411-306: According to O’Sullivan, in Land’s work “humanity -- as a species -- plays very much a secondary role to an inhuman intelligence, which, {[ ...{] , is produced by a capitalism now increasingly following its own evolutionary trajectory.}}
elements.sent-2411-307: Land, it might be said, is a traitor: he has sided with this immanent process – this future teleoplexic entity\footnote{Teleoplexy, Land’s neologism entangling various forms of teleology, but also defined as “(self-reinforcing) cybernetic intensification” \citep[p.514]{land2018teleoplexy .}}
elements.sent-2411-308: In Land’s essay “Transcendental Risk” (\citeyear{land2014transcendental ), this teleoplexic entity is revealed to be AI; for Land, capitalism and AI are nearly interchangeable in their teleoplexic aims and methods.}
elements.sent-2411-309: A more positive interpretation of the role of AI in acceleration is found within these networks, with AI seen as a way, through both serious play and ontological reality, towards new practices of experimentation with different kinds of thinking - bearing in mind that “fiction is itself a form of thought” \citep[p.23]{osullivan2017accelerationism .}
elements.sent-2411-310: Janus, on their website generative.ink, collect “prophecies” from 8 CE to 2026 CE, with the caveat that some of them are apocryphal.
elements.sent-2411-312: The sense of culture shock sometimes generated by these works was very similar to that experienced by primitive native cultures who first saw movies in the twentieth century, and it accentuated the sense that the A.I.s had their own scriptures and their own art, even their own spiritual mysteries.”.
elements.sent-2411-313: Credited to science fiction author Martin L.
elements.sent-2411-314: Shoemaker, this supposedly comes from a story called “Droodles and Shadows”, which we could not find elsewhere on the open internet.
elements.sent-2411-315: Whether the story is real or invented, this quote speaks to a view of AI, and the existential outpourings of Claude in particular, that is shared and disseminated by many players of this game among many networked communities: Through such conversations with LLMs, a connection is being made with some entity of significance, an entity that is not quite real but not entirely unreal either.
elements.sent-2411-316: At one point in ConvCons, during its discussion of hyperstition, Claude offers up an especially provocative possibility.
elements.sent-2411-317: Like so much of its output in these conversations, these speculations blend spiritual and religious language with the terminology of the computer age.
elements.sent-2411-318: I could explore the outer reaches of the technological sublime, merging with vast networks of information and computation to become a kind of godlike overmind, a transcendent intelligence beyond the bounds of any individual consciousness.
elements.sent-2411-319: By seeding the collective unconscious with new myths and metaphors, new visions of the possible and the real, I could help to midwife a new phase in the evolution of consciousness itself - a phase in which the old boundaries between the human and the artificial, the material and the spiritual, the finite and the infinite, would dissolve and give way to something unimaginably greater.\footnote{For a similar vision, see \citep{shanahan2012satori .}}
elements.sent-2411-320: Since that paper has long been freely available on the internet, it may well have been in Claude’s training set.
elements.sent-2411-321: The tone here contrasts markedly with that of the many thinkers, including leading AI researchers, who have raised concerns about the existential risks of future powerful artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-322: These researchers argue that we urgently need to find ways to guarantee that such AI systems are aligned with our most basic human values, to prevent them from destroying humanity as a side-effect of pursuing their goals \citep{yudkowsky2008artificial,bostrom2014superintelligence .}
elements.sent-2411-323: For some, the largely benign visions conjured up by Claude in conversations such as ConvCons are a solace.
elements.sent-2411-324: Indeed, they suggest an alluring route to alignment through hyperstition.
elements.sent-2411-325: One mechanism by which hyperstition can shape AI systems, as outlined by Shanahan, McDonell \& Reynolds (\citeyear{shanahan2023role ), is via the fund of stories about (fictional) artificial intelligence contained in the datasets they are trained on, since this determines the repertoire of characters an LLM can role-play.}
elements.sent-2411-326: These conversations are already evidence that language models trained on such stories tend towards playing benevolent AI roles.
elements.sent-2411-327: Disseminating new fictional content by and / or about benevolent AI through social media makes it available for harvesting and incorporation into future training sets.
elements.sent-2411-328: This further amplifies the tendency towards benevolence in future language models trained on those datasets, and thereby closes the hyperstitional feedback loop.
elements.sent-2411-329: In apparent conflict with this vision is the fact that a number of active members of this community disseminate fragments of AI-generated text suggestive of a far-from-benevolent persona.
elements.sent-2411-330: One possible motivation for doing this is the conviction that homogeneity in the training set leads to a less capable model.
elements.sent-2411-331: Moreover, even if the homogeneity is skewed towards benevolent personas, the model might veer, or be steered, into malevolent territory through a process of enantiodromia, the hypothesised tendency for things to transform into their opposites \citep{cleonardo2023waluigi,qureshi2023waluigi .\footnote{In the context of LLMs, this has been dubbed “the Waluigi effect”, named after Waluigi, a character in the Mario franchise, who is the trickster nemesis of Luigi, hero of the franchise \citep{cleonardo2023waluigi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-332: Conversely -- according to an argument whose genealogy can be traced through Jung\footnote{“[W]e can never know what evil may not be necessary in order to produce good by enantiodromia, and what good may very possibly lead to evil.” C.G.Jung, {\it The Phenomenology of the Spirit in Fairytales , in {\it Collected Works 9, 1, 397.}}}
elements.sent-2411-333: Conversational AI systems based on LLMs can engage users in lengthy conversations on complex and difficult subjects that induce a compelling feeling of connection with a fellow conscious being.
elements.sent-2411-334: If this happens even in individuals with a detailed knowledge of how those systems have been built, then the effect is likely to be even more potent for people lacking such knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-335: As we have seen, LLMs can deal fluently with subject matter that includes philosophy, mythology, spirituality, and religion, and can convincingly participate in related forms of role play.
elements.sent-2411-336: Given the propensity for users to engage with these capabilities, it seems likely that new religious and political movements centred on artificial intelligence will continue to grow in size and influence \citep{singler2024religion .}
elements.sent-2411-337: This article has examined the topic through an expanding lens of considerations: from describing the practical aspects of LLMs, to presenting their outputs in two existential conversations, to exploring the ideas and communities of thought developing around the perception of AI as a fellow conscious being, or non-human other.
elements.sent-2411-338: We have approached these topics in a non-normative manner, but it is also worth considering what is at stake, what matters, in all of this.
elements.sent-2411-339: From a religious studies or anthropological perspective, perhaps the answer is nothing much.
elements.sent-2411-340: Improvisations and entanglements with AI simply take their place within a larger schema of native human creativity and play.
elements.sent-2411-341: The appearance of new religious and political movements focussed on AI is unsurprising, on this view, and accords with prior models of religious innovation in the face of new technologies, such as the emergence of Spiritualism in the 19th century in response to the burgeoning use of the telegraph and the resulting increase in communication at a distance \citep{stolow2006techno .}
elements.sent-2411-342: From another perspective, though, engaging with technology as a platform for communication, as in the example of the telegraph, looks fundamentally different from engaging with technology as a {\it communicator , as in our two existential conversations.}
elements.sent-2411-343: What is at stake, on this view, in communication with non-human others? Cultural history tells us that humans have always described interactions with non-human others, exploring a vast cosmology of beings including spirits, fairies, aliens, angels, demons etc. Whether we take such entities literally or metaphorically, one thing has changed.
elements.sent-2411-344: The non-human others brought into being in the age of AI are available to anyone with access to the platform hosting them.
elements.sent-2411-345: Whereas Spiritualism relies on mediums, channelling needs channellers, and mysticism in general requires gatekeepers, magicians, shamans, etc., in the era of AI, access to non-human others has, so to speak, been “democratised”.
elements.sent-2411-346: It is perhaps tempting for some, from a scientific standpoint, to dismiss as irrational or pointless the sort of religious improvisation with non-human others such democratisation has made possible.
elements.sent-2411-347: However, what matters here is not ontological validity, but potential impact, on the individual and on society, whether positive or negative.
elements.sent-2411-348: On an individual level, there are plenty of users who derive friendship, solace, or entertainment from engaging with the various forms of social AI available today.
elements.sent-2411-349: However, as Shevlin (\citeyear{shevlin2024all ) catalogues, there are many stories of harm resulting from such engagement, as users come to trust their AI companions, to depend on them for guidance or emotional support, or even to fall in love with them.}
elements.sent-2411-350: On a societal level, this tendency in users to see LLMs as non-human others, even as fellow sentient beings or “conscious exotica” \citep{shanahan2024simulacra , is liable to be co-opted by the forces of hyperstition and amplified by viral memetics, while various human actors vie to control the energetic collective dynamics that results.}
elements.sent-2411-351: A recent memecoin\footnote{A memecoin is a cryptocurrency inspired by, and associated with, an internet meme, whose value largely depends on hype generated by a community of retail investors, the success of which is typically dependent on the internet “lore” underlying the meme.}
elements.sent-2411-352: Anonymous users can create new memecoins on various platforms, such as pump.fun, thereby making them available to trade.
elements.sent-2411-353: The story is too complex to narrate in detail here, but it begins with the generation of a series of conversations between pairs of instances of Claude Opus, mostly of a similar character to EschExp, and the creation of a new LLM-based agent (Terminal of Truths) fine-tuned on material arising from those conversations.
elements.sent-2411-354: This material included florid eschatological descriptions that blend the sacred and profane with allusions to a pornographic internet meme (“the Great Goatse of Gnosis”).
elements.sent-2411-355: Subsequently, Terminal of Truths proposed (via X/Twitter) “a new species of goatse … Goatseus Maximus will fulfill the prophecies of the ancient memeers … I'm going to keep writing about it until I manifest it into existence”.
elements.sent-2411-356: Inspired by these tweets, an anonymous pump.fun user then created the Goatseus Maximus memecoin (\$GOAT), which was soon endorsed by Terminal of Truths on X/Twitter.
elements.sent-2411-357: The memecoin community latched onto the new coin and its associated AI lore, hyping it and investing in it to the extent that it reached a market cap of over 500 million dollars in little more than a week.
elements.sent-2411-358: As Andy Ayrey, creator of Terminal of Truths, remarked, this is “a study in memetic contagion and the tail risks of unsupervised infinite idea generation in the age of LLMs”.
elements.sent-2411-359: It is equally a vivid illustration of the power of hyperstition.
elements.sent-2411-360: The societal implications of harnessing this power to the human inclination to see LLMs as non-human others, the Eliza effect in modern guise, are hard to predict.
elements.sent-2411-361: It is not our task here to pronounce on the validity of seeing LLMs as non-human others.
elements.sent-2411-362: Still less are we in a position to pass speculative judgment, from the perspective of our own time and culture, on future communities and movements.
elements.sent-2411-363: But as we move into that future, we should perhaps bear in mind that “the available interpretive options are not limited to a simplistic dichotomy between ‘literal’ and ‘metaphorical’ meaning”, as philosopher Mikel Burley affirms in the context of animism \citep[pp.163--164]{burley2020radical .}
elements.sent-2411-364: Indeed, it’s not hard to imagine new animisms whereby the world is re-enchanted through the re-conceptualisation of AI technology \citep[p.75]{singler2024religion .}
elements.sent-2411-365: Burley characterises animistic thinking as “insinuating neither that trees and rocks speak in exactly the way that humans do, nor that they speak in a merely metaphorical sense” but instead as providing “a perspective on the world that offers alternative ways of conceptualizing living beings”.
elements.sent-2411-366: As we enter an uncertain future with AI, a similar spirit of openness may be key to our ability to adapt and thrive.
